title: Voronoi diagrams and nearest-neighbour problems
summary: A Voronoi diagram takes a scatter of fixed points, called sites, and carves the whole plane into territories, one per site, so that every location belongs to the site it is closest to. This lesson builds the full 3.4 picture: the three ingredients of the diagram (cells, edges and vertices) and exactly what each one means in terms of distance; how to construct a simple diagram by drawing perpendicular bisectors; how to find the equation of a single edge from coordinate geometry; nearest-neighbour interpolation, where an unknown value is estimated by copying the value of the closest site; and the toxic-waste or largest-empty-circle problem, whose optimal point always sits at a Voronoi vertex. Applications run from the nearest hospital or school to signal coverage. Every worked example is laid out exactly the way our marking engine awards the marks, with method marks, dependent accuracy marks and follow-through.
learningObjectives: State precisely what a cell, an edge and a vertex of a Voronoi diagram represent in terms of distance to the sites.
Construct a simple Voronoi diagram by finding perpendicular bisectors, and find the equation of a single edge from the coordinates of two sites.
Use nearest-neighbour interpolation to assign an unknown location the value of its closest site.
Solve the toxic-waste / largest-empty-circle problem, recognising that the optimal point is a Voronoi vertex and finding the radius as the distance from that vertex to a nearby site.
Apply Voronoi diagrams to real contexts (nearest hospital or school, signal coverage) and present working so that method marks and follow-through protect the final answer.

simpleExplanation.title: Dividing a Map by Who Is Closest
simpleExplanation.summary: A Voronoi diagram starts with a handful of fixed points, called sites, and paints the map so that every location is coloured by the site nearest to it. Each colour is a cell; the borders between colours are edges; the corners where three colours meet are vertices. That single idea, closest site wins, answers a surprising range of questions.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Picture several fire stations in a city. If a fire breaks out, the nearest station responds. Draw the region each station covers and you have partitioned the whole city into response zones, one per station. Stand anywhere inside a zone and its station is your closest. Stand on a border and two stations are tied for nearest. Stand on a corner where three zones meet and three stations are all exactly the same distance away. That map of zones is a Voronoi diagram.
steps: Start with the fixed points, the sites (fire stations, hospitals, phone masts).

For each pair of neighbouring sites draw the perpendicular bisector of the segment joining them; the part that forms a real border is an edge, and every point on it is equidistant from those two sites.
Where edges meet you get a vertex, a point equidistant from three sites at once.
The region enclosed around a single site is its cell: every point inside is closer to that site than to any other.

flashcards.back: One of the fixed starting points that generates the diagram, for example a hospital, a school or a phone mast. Sites are given data; you do not calculate them. Every cell belongs to exactly one site.
The region belonging to one site: every point inside it is closer to that site than to any other site. A point on the plane belongs to the cell of its nearest site.

A boundary segment between two neighbouring cells. Every point on an edge is equidistant from the two sites whose cells it separates, so an edge is part of the perpendicular bisector of the segment joining those two sites.
A point where three (or more) edges meet. A vertex is equidistant from the three sites whose cells surround it, so it is the same distance from three sites at once.

The set of all points equidistant from two fixed points A and B is exactly the perpendicular bisector of segment [AB]. The border between A's cell and B's cell is where distances tie, so it lies on that perpendicular bisector.

flashcards.front: Equation of an edge between A and B
flashcards.back: 1) Midpoint M of [AB]. 2) Gradient $m_{AB}$ of [AB]. 3) Perpendicular gradient $m_\perp = -\dfrac{1}{m_{AB}}$. 4) Line through M with gradient $m_\perp$: $y - y_M = m_\perp(x - x_M)$.

To estimate an unknown value at a location, give it the value of the site whose cell it falls in, its nearest site. Constant within each cell, it jumps at the edges. Simple and fast, but ignores how far away that nearest site is.

To place a new point as far as possible from all existing sites inside the region, use a Voronoi vertex: it is a local farthest point because it is equidistant from three sites. The radius of the largest empty circle is the distance from that vertex to any one of its three sites.

flashcards.front: Radius of the largest empty circle
flashcards.back: Once the optimal vertex V is found, the radius is the distance from V to any one of the three equidistant sites, using $d = \sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2}$. All three give the same value, which is a useful check.

Sites are the given points (hospitals, masts); vertices are calculated corners where three cells meet. A vertex is equidistant from three sites and is the candidate location for the toxic-waste or largest-empty-circle problem, whereas a site is just a starting location.

flashcards.front: Trap: edge is not the whole perpendicular bisector
flashcards.back: An edge lies ON the perpendicular bisector of two sites, but only the portion that actually separates the two cells is the edge; the rest of the infinite line is not part of the diagram. In coordinate questions you still quote the full line equation unless asked for the segment.

Voronoi cells use straight-line (Euclidean) distance. 'Nearest hospital' on a Voronoi diagram means nearest as the crow flies, which need not be the nearest by road. Note this whenever a real context could involve travel routes.

quickCheck.prompt: An edge of a Voronoi diagram between two sites A and B is:
options: The perpendicular bisector of the segment [AB]
The line segment [AB] itself
The line through A and B
A circle centred on the midpoint of [AB]
quickCheck.answer: The perpendicular bisector of the segment [AB]

quickCheck.prompt: The cell belonging to a site P consists of all points that are:
options: Closer to P than to any other site
Exactly equidistant from P and one other site
Further from P than from any other site
At a fixed distance from P
quickCheck.answer: Closer to P than to any other site

quickCheck.prompt: A vertex of a Voronoi diagram is a point that is:
options: Equidistant from three sites
Equidistant from exactly two sites
One of the original sites
The midpoint of two sites

quickCheck.prompt: A toxic-waste dump must be placed as far as possible from every town, where the towns are the sites. Its optimal location (inside the region) is:
options: At a Voronoi vertex
At a site
On the midpoint of the two closest towns
Anywhere on an edge

quickCheck.prompt: Using nearest-neighbour interpolation, the estimated value at a point is:
options: The value of the site whose cell contains the point
The average of all the site values
The value of the site at the nearest vertex
The distance-weighted mean of the three closest sites
quickCheck.answer: The value of the site whose cell contains the point

faq.q: What is the difference between a site and a vertex?
faq.a: A site is one of the original data points you are given, such as a hospital or a phone mast. A vertex is a calculated point where three edges (and therefore three cells) meet. The vertex is special because it is equidistant from those three sites, which makes it the natural candidate for the largest-empty-circle or toxic-waste problem. Sites are inputs; vertices are outputs you compute.

faq.q: Why is an edge always a perpendicular bisector?
faq.a: An edge separates the cell of site A from the cell of site B, so it is made of points that are equally close to A and to B. The set of all points equidistant from two fixed points is, by definition, the perpendicular bisector of the segment joining them. So the edge must lie on that perpendicular bisector. Only the part that genuinely borders the two cells is drawn, but its equation is the perpendicular bisector's equation.

faq.q: Do I need to draw the diagram perfectly to scale in the exam?
faq.a: No. A clear, well-labelled sketch is enough. The marks come from the calculations, finding midpoints, gradients, equations of edges, intersection points for vertices and distances, not from artistic accuracy. Show the method for each of these and the sketch simply supports your reasoning.

faq.q: Why is the best toxic-waste location a vertex and not somewhere on an edge?
faq.a: Moving along an edge, you stay equidistant from two sites but your distance to a third site keeps changing, so you can usually keep getting further from the whole set by sliding towards a vertex. A vertex is where three edges meet and you are equidistant from three sites at once; you can no longer increase the distance to all of them by a small move. That local balance is why the optimal point inside the region is a vertex.

On a standard Voronoi diagram, yes, distance is straight-line (Euclidean). That matters in real contexts: the nearest hospital as the crow flies may not be the nearest by road if a river or motorway is in the way. In the exam, work with straight-line distance unless told otherwise, but be ready to comment that real travel distance can differ.

sections.content: Give a delivery company a list of depots and it will want to know, for any address, which depot is closest. Give a health authority a list of hospitals and it will want to map which neighbourhoods each hospital serves. Both questions have the same answer: a Voronoi diagram, which partitions the whole plane into territories so that every point belongs to its nearest site. This lesson covers the full 3.4 picture, the meaning of cells, edges and vertices, constructing a simple diagram, finding the equation of an edge, nearest-neighbour interpolation, and the toxic-waste / largest-empty-circle problem whose answer is always a vertex. As in every calculation topic, the worked examples below are laid out exactly the way our engine marks your own practice.
The three ingredients: cells, edges and vertices

A Voronoi diagram begins with a set of fixed points called **sites**, for example the locations of hospitals, schools or phone masts. From them the plane is split into **cells**, one per site. The cell of a site $P$ contains every point that is closer to $P$ than to any other site, so if you are standing somewhere in $P$'s cell then $P$ is your nearest site. The borders between cells are **edges**, and the corners where edges meet are **vertices**. Everything about the diagram flows from one idea: each feature is defined by distance to the sites.

items: **Site:** a fixed given point (a hospital, a mast). You do not calculate sites; they are the input.
**Cell:** the region around a site containing every point closer to that site than to any other. One cell per site.
**Edge:** a boundary between two cells. Every point on it is equidistant from the two sites it separates, so it lies on their perpendicular bisector.
**Vertex:** a corner where three (or more) edges meet. It is equidistant from the three sites whose cells meet there.

sections.content: It is worth pausing on why an edge is a perpendicular bisector. An edge separates the cell of $A$ from the cell of $B$, so it is made of exactly those points that are equally close to $A$ and to $B$. The set of all points equidistant from two fixed points is precisely the perpendicular bisector of the segment joining them. That single fact, equidistant from two points equals perpendicular bisector, is the engine behind every construction in this topic.
Constructing a simple diagram

To construct a Voronoi diagram by hand you draw the perpendicular bisector of each pair of neighbouring sites and keep only the parts that genuinely form borders. For two sites there is a single dividing line. For three sites you draw three perpendicular bisectors; they meet at one vertex, and the three edges radiating from it split the plane into three cells. Adding more sites simply repeats the idea: bisect neighbouring pairs, find where the bisectors cross, and rub out the pieces of each bisector that lie inside a cell where a different site is actually closest.

The algebra you need is just coordinate geometry. The edge between two sites $A(x_A, y_A)$ and $B(x_B, y_B)$ is the perpendicular bisector of $[AB]$, so you need its midpoint and the perpendicular gradient.

Midpoint of $[AB]$: $M = \left( \dfrac{x_A + x_B}{2}, \dfrac{y_A + y_B}{2} ight)$ 
 Gradient of $[AB]$: $m_{AB} = \dfrac{y_B - y_A}{x_B - x_A}$ 
 Perpendicular gradient: $m_\perp = -\dfrac{1}{m_{AB}}$ 
 Equation of the edge: $y - y_M = m_\perp (x - x_M)$
Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

In Paper 1 the marks are analytic: each is tied to a specific line of working, either a method mark (M) or an accuracy mark (A), and an accuracy mark depends on the method mark it follows. Follow-through (FT) means an earlier slip need not cost you the marks that depend on it, provided the later step is done correctly on your own figures. But that protection only exists if the method is on the page. Study how each mark below is earned by a specific line.

sections.question: Two sites are at A(2, 1) and B(6, 5). Find the equation of the Voronoi edge between their cells. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer — full working.**

The edge between the cells of $A$ and $B$ is the perpendicular bisector of $[AB]$, the set of points equidistant from $A$ and $B$.

**Midpoint of [AB].**
$M = \left( \dfrac{2 + 6}{2}, \dfrac{1 + 5}{2} ight) = (4, 3).$

**Gradient of [AB] and the perpendicular gradient.**
$m_{AB} = \dfrac{5 - 1}{6 - 2} = \dfrac{4}{4} = 1,$ so the perpendicular gradient is $m_\perp = -\dfrac{1}{1} = -1.$

**Equation of the edge.** Through $M(4, 3)$ with gradient $-1$:
$y - 3 = -1(x - 4).$

**Simplify.**
$y - 3 = -x + 4 \;\Rightarrow\; y = -x + 7.$

**Edge:** $y = -x + 7$ (equivalently $x + y = 7$).

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks:**

- **M1 — midpoint.** A method mark for correctly forming the midpoint $M = (4, 3)$ of $[AB]$. It is the approach that is rewarded, so it survives an arithmetic slip further down.
- **M1 — perpendicular gradient.** A method mark for finding $m_{AB} = 1$ and taking the negative reciprocal to get $m_\perp = -1$. The engine checks that you took the perpendicular (negative reciprocal) gradient, not the gradient of $[AB]$ itself.
- **A1 — equation.** An accuracy mark for a correct equation of the line through the midpoint with the perpendicular gradient, e.g. $y - 3 = -1(x - 4)$. This A-mark depends on both M-marks above and is earned once the correct point and gradient are combined. FT applies: a candidate whose midpoint or gradient differed but who substituted their own values correctly still earns it.
- **A1 — simplified form.** An accuracy mark for the tidy final form $y = -x + 7$ (or any equivalent, such as $x + y = 7$).

**'Accept equivalent forms.'** The engine accepts $y = -x + 7$, $y = 7 - x$ and $x + y = 7$ as the same edge. Once a correct final equation appears, subsequent restatements do not lose marks (ISW).

**Bottom line:** of the 4 marks, two are method marks that survive an arithmetic slip, and the two accuracy marks are shielded by follow-through, but only if the midpoint, the perpendicular gradient and the substitution are all written down.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR working exactly this way, analytically, mark by mark, with method marks, dependent accuracy marks and follow-through. So always show the method: state the midpoint, compute the gradient of $[AB]$, take the negative reciprocal for the perpendicular gradient, substitute into the line equation, then simplify. The method marks and follow-through are what protect your score when a number slips, and you only get that protection if the working is on the page.
Finding a vertex and the largest empty circle

A vertex is where two edges cross, so you find it by solving the two edge equations simultaneously. Because a vertex is equidistant from three sites, it answers the classic optimisation question: where should a new facility go so that it is as far as possible from every existing site? This is the **largest-empty-circle** or **toxic-waste** problem, place a hazardous dump as far as possible from all towns, or a new mast as far as possible from all existing masts to avoid interference. Inside the region, the optimal point is always a Voronoi vertex, and the radius of the largest empty circle is the distance from that vertex to any one of its three equidistant sites.

In Paper 1 you have your GDC. Use the simultaneous-equation solver to find the intersection of two edges (the vertex), but always write down the two equations you are solving so the method mark is on the page, even when the GDC produces the final coordinates.

sections.question:
Three phone masts stand at A(1, 5), B(7, 7) and C(9, 1). 
 (a) Find the equation of the Voronoi edge between the cells of A and B. 
 (b) The edge between the cells of B and C is $y = x - 2$. Find the coordinates of the Voronoi vertex. 
 (c) A new emergency antenna must be placed as far as possible from all three masts. State its optimal location and, to 3 significant figures, find its distance from the masts. [8]
sections.solution:
**(a) Edge between A and B.** This is the perpendicular bisector of $[AB]$.
$M_{AB} = \left( \dfrac{1+7}{2}, \dfrac{5+7}{2} ight) = (4, 6).$  [M1: midpoint]
$m_{AB} = \dfrac{7-5}{7-1} = \dfrac{2}{6} = \dfrac{1}{3}, \qquad m_\perp = -3.$  [M1: perpendicular gradient]
Through $(4, 6)$ with gradient $-3$: $y - 6 = -3(x - 4)$, so $y = -3x + 18.$  [A1: equation]

**(b) Vertex.** The vertex is where the A|B edge meets the B|C edge, so solve simultaneously:
$y = -3x + 18 \quad 	ext{and} \quad y = x - 2.$  [M1: set up simultaneous equations]
$-3x + 18 = x - 2 \;\Rightarrow\; 20 = 4x \;\Rightarrow\; x = 5,$ then $y = 5 - 2 = 3.$
Vertex at $(5, 3)$, equidistant from A, B and C.  [A1]

**(c) Optimal antenna.** To be as far as possible from all three masts, place the antenna at the Voronoi vertex $(5, 3)$.  [A1: location]
The distance is the radius of the largest empty circle, from $V(5, 3)$ to any site, say $A(1, 5)$:
$d = \sqrt{(5-1)^2 + (3-5)^2} = \sqrt{16 + 4} = \sqrt{20}.$  [M1: distance from vertex to a site]
$d = 4.4721\ldots = 4.47$ (3 s.f.).  [A1]
Check with $B(7, 7)$: $\sqrt{(5-7)^2 + (3-7)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 16} = \sqrt{20}$, the same, as expected for a vertex.

sections.content: Nearest-neighbour interpolation

Sometimes each site carries a value, a weather station records a temperature, a sensor records a pollution level, and you want to estimate the value at a location with no station. **Nearest-neighbour interpolation** does the simplest possible thing: it gives the location the value of its nearest site, that is, the value of the site whose Voronoi cell contains it. So the Voronoi diagram is exactly the map of which value applies where. The estimate is constant across each cell and jumps at the edges. It is quick and needs no weighting, but it ignores how far the nearest site actually is and takes no account of the second-nearest site, so it can be crude near the borders.

sections.question: Three weather stations record midday temperatures: P(0, 0) reads 18 °C, Q(10, 0) reads 22 °C and R(5, 8) reads 15 °C. Using nearest-neighbour interpolation, estimate the temperature at the point T(4, 1). [4]
sections.solution:
Nearest-neighbour interpolation gives $T$ the temperature of its nearest station, so compare the distances from $T(4,1)$ to each station. (Comparing squared distances is enough and avoids surds.)

$PT^2 = (4-0)^2 + (1-0)^2 = 16 + 1 = 17.$  [M1: distances to each site]
$QT^2 = (4-10)^2 + (1-0)^2 = 36 + 1 = 37.$
$RT^2 = (4-5)^2 + (1-8)^2 = 1 + 49 = 50.$  [A1: all three correct]

The smallest is $PT^2 = 17$, so $P(0,0)$ is the nearest station.  [M1: identify nearest]

Therefore the estimated temperature at $T$ is that of station $P$: $18\ ^\circ	ext{C}$.  [A1]

Because the method copies the nearest station's reading, the estimate is $18\ ^\circ	ext{C}$ everywhere inside P's cell, and would jump the instant $T$ crossed an edge into another station's cell.

sections.content: Applications: nearest facility and coverage

The everyday power of Voronoi diagrams is answering 'which one is nearest?' at a glance. Overlay the diagram for a set of hospitals on a map and every neighbourhood is instantly colour-coded by the hospital that serves it; the same works for schools, fire stations or supermarket depots. Signal coverage is the mirror image: if each mast reaches a fixed radius, the cells tell you which mast a phone should connect to, and comparing a cell's size to that radius reveals gaps where no mast reaches, a coverage hole. Two cautions carry real marks. First, Voronoi 'nearest' is straight-line distance, which can differ from travel distance by road. Second, the diagram assumes the sites are the only thing that matters; a real decision might also weigh capacity or opening hours.

When a context question asks for the 'nearest hospital' to a point, you usually do not need the whole diagram, just decide which cell the point is in. Compare the distance from the point to each candidate site (squared distances are enough) and pick the smallest. Show those distance comparisons: that is where the method marks live.
Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Using the segment [AB] or the line through A and B as the edge** — the edge is the PERPENDICULAR BISECTOR of [AB]. Find the midpoint and the negative-reciprocal gradient; do not quote the gradient of [AB] itself.
**Forgetting the negative reciprocal** — the edge's gradient is $-1/m_{AB}$, not $m_{AB}$. A perpendicular-gradient slip loses the accuracy marks that depend on it.
**Confusing a site with a vertex** — the toxic-waste / largest-empty-circle answer is a VERTEX (equidistant from three sites), never a site. Solve two edge equations simultaneously to find it.

**Measuring the radius from the wrong point** — the largest empty circle is centred at the vertex; its radius is the distance from that VERTEX to a site, not the distance between two sites or from a midpoint.

**Weighting or averaging in nearest-neighbour interpolation** — nearest-neighbour copies the single nearest site's value; it does not average the neighbours or weight by distance. Compare distances and take the closest.
**Not showing the simultaneous equations for a vertex** — even when the GDC finds the intersection, write down the two edge equations you solved, or the method mark is lost.
**Assuming 'nearest' means nearest by road** — Voronoi cells use straight-line distance; note the distinction whenever a real route could differ.

sections.content: Where this leads

Voronoi diagrams tie the coordinate geometry of 3.1 to 3.3, midpoints, gradients, perpendicular lines and the distance formula, into a single tool for reasoning about proximity. The habit they build, define a feature by distance and then compute it, is exactly the discipline every applied geometry question rewards: state what is equidistant, form the perpendicular bisector, solve for the vertex, measure the radius. Master that chain and the optimisation flavour of the toxic-waste problem generalises far beyond masts and hospitals, to any situation where the best location is the one that balances the distances to a set of fixed points.

sections.label: Get a Paper 2 question marked: find a Voronoi edge, a vertex and the largest empty circle with full working
